---
title: "Inputs"
---

This page defines the supported input contract for `IMU-GPS-ESKF`. The app is written against the verified fact-patterns documented here rather than against a general-purpose runtime validation layer for arbitrary JSON. Every factual claim on this page has been checked against the supported input files.

## Supported input sets

### Bundled simulated pair

`IMU-GPS-ESKF` supports the bundled `scene_pose.json` and `scene_ms_imu.json` files. These files were generated by simulation to follow the same input fact-patterns documented on this page.

### nuScenes CAN-bus scene pairs

`IMU-GPS-ESKF` also supports nuScenes CAN-bus scene pairs named `scene-XXXX_pose.json` and `scene-XXXX_ms_imu.json`, released in February 2020. [@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-api; @nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

The upstream nuScenes CAN-bus download stores these files under `nuscenes/can_bus/`. Using nuScenes input requires a separate nuScenes download. [@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-api]

### nuScenes scene inventory

The nuScenes CAN-bus download provides `979` scene pairs. `scene-0001_pose.json` and `scene-0001_ms_imu.json` are the pair for scene `0001`. Scene IDs are sparse and range from `scene-0001` through `scene-1110`. [@nuscenes-devkit-root-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-api; @nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

### nuScenes file-name variation

The CAN-bus README prose sometimes says `scene_XXXX_imu.json`, but the API code and examples use `scene-XXXX_ms_imu.json`. [@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-api]

## Runtime pair selection

At runtime, the app selects the active pose/IMU pair from `scenarios`.

Selection follows this rule:

- if exactly one matching nuScenes pair is present, the app uses that pair
- otherwise, the bundled simulated pair supplies the default run

## Shared file format

### JSON container

Each supported input file is a JSON array. [@nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

### Quaternion-valued fields

Quaternion-valued fields follow the quaternion component order defined on `Conventions`.

### Units

Units are SI unless a file-specific section states otherwise.

## Shared time properties

### Timestamp representation

Each record includes integer microsecond `utime`. [@nuscenes-canbus-readme]

### Time ordering

Within each file, records are in strictly increasing `utime` order. Across all `979` checked `pose` files and all `979` checked `ms_imu` files, no violations were observed. [@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

### Shared time axis

Within a scene pair, pose and IMU timestamps refer to the same time axis.

### Sampling cadence

`pose` is approximately `50 Hz` and `ms_imu` is approximately `100 Hz`, but neither stream lies on an exact `20000 us` or `10000 us` grid. Actual timestamps are irregular. Small jitter and occasional larger gaps are expected in real data. [@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

## Pose input (`*_pose.json`)

### Purpose in `IMU-GPS-ESKF`

This file provides the reference pose stream used for truth lookup, checks, and evaluation. [@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @caesar2020nuscenes; @nuscenes-issue-1111]

### nuScenes provenance

For nuScenes scenes, the pose stream comes from the nuScenes localization pipeline: offline lidar HD map construction followed by online Monte Carlo Localization with lidar and odometry. Reported localization error is `<= 10 cm`. This localization output is the pose stream. [@caesar2020nuscenes; @nuscenes-canbus-readme]

### Example record

```json
[
  {
    "utime": 1700000000000000,
    "pos": [120.0, -45.0, 0.0],
    "orientation": [0.9939560979566968, 0.0, 0.0, 0.10977830083717481],
    "vel": [8.272592839624389, 0.0, 0.0],
    "accel": [1.4902411831299034, 6.58109833210669e-14, 9.8],
    "rotation_rate": [0.0, 0.0, 7.95530308295156e-15]
  }
]
```

### Record schema

Across all `979` checked `pose` files, every record uses the exact key set `accel`, `orientation`, `pos`, `rotation_rate`, `utime`, `vel`. Vector lengths are always:

- `pos`: length `3`
- `orientation`: length `4`
- `vel`: length `3`
- `accel`: length `3`
- `rotation_rate`: length `3` [@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

### Field semantics

- `utime`: integer timestamp in microseconds
- `pos`: global-frame position in meters [@nuscenes-canbus-readme]
- `orientation`: orientation quaternion
- `vel`: ego-frame velocity in $\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}$ [@nuscenes-canbus-readme]
- `accel`: ego-frame acceleration in $\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^2$ [@nuscenes-canbus-readme]
- `rotation_rate`: ego-frame angular velocity in $\mathrm{rad}/\mathrm{s}$ [@nuscenes-canbus-readme]

### Position structure

Across `952,103` checked pose records, `pos` has exact planar form `pos = [x, y, 0]`. [@nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

### Orientation structure

Across the same checked pose records, `orientation` has exact yaw-only form `orientation = [w, 0, 0, z]`. Pose quaternions are unit length to machine precision. [@nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

### Velocity structure

Across the same checked pose records, `vel` has exact forward-only form `vel = [forward, 0, 0]`. [@nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

### Dynamic-channel statistics

Despite the planar and yaw-only structure above, the ego-frame dynamic channels are nontrivial. In checked pose records, `pose.accel[2] mean/med/min/max = 9.784/9.789/4.818/15.063`, and `|pose.rotation_rate[0]|, |pose.rotation_rate[1]|, |pose.rotation_rate[2]| med/max = (0.0074, 0.0079, 0.0086)/(0.3223, 0.4490, 0.6947)` $\mathrm{rad}/\mathrm{s}$. [@nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

## IMU input (`*_ms_imu.json`)

### Purpose in `IMU-GPS-ESKF`

This file provides the IMU stream used for initialization and propagation.

### Example record

```json
[
  {
    "utime": 1700000000000000,
    "linear_accel": [1.543318572672857, -0.03916157510402117, 9.804608745997307],
    "rotation_rate": [-0.0018336573502681383, -0.0014580203316534287, -0.001036707959881021],
    "q": [0.9568021423210139, 0.0, 0.0, 0.29073985012364273]
  }
]
```

### Record schema

Across all `979` checked `ms_imu` files, every record uses the exact key set `linear_accel`, `q`, `rotation_rate`, `utime`. Vector lengths are always:

- `linear_accel`: length `3`
- `q`: length `4`
- `rotation_rate`: length `3` [@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

### Field semantics

- `utime`: integer timestamp in microseconds
- `linear_accel`: specific force in frame `I`, in $\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}^2$ [@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-linear-accel-specific-force-local-2026-03-26]
- `rotation_rate`: angular velocity in frame `I`, in $\mathrm{rad}/\mathrm{s}$ [@nuscenes-canbus-readme]
- `q`: IMU-to-gravity-aligned orientation quaternion ${}^{A}q_{\fsub{I}}$; frame names and transform notation are defined on `Conventions` [@nuscenes-canbus-readme]

### Specific-force convention

After rotating `linear_accel` into the gravity-aligned frame defined by `q` and restricting to near-stationary moments, samples cluster near `[0, 0, 9.8]`, not `[0, 0, 0]`. In `147006` matched stationary samples, the mean is `[0.00016, 0.00224, 9.79705]` and the median is `[-0.00007, 0.00126, 9.79619]`. [@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-linear-accel-specific-force-local-2026-03-26; @nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

### Quaternion normalization

IMU `q` is essentially unit length. The maximum observed norm error is about `1.36e-7`. [@nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

### Quaternion tilt content

IMU `q` is not pure yaw. Observed `|q[1]|, |q[2]| med/mean/max = (0.0071, 0.0080)/(0.0098, 0.0101)/(0.0712, 0.0701)`. [@nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

### Acceleration dimensionality

`linear_accel` is genuinely 3D. Observed `|linear_accel[0]|, |linear_accel[1]| med/mean/max = (0.389, 0.241)/(0.522, 0.349)/(5.491, 4.541)`, and `linear_accel[2] mean/med/min/max = 9.784/9.786/-2.802/21.610`. [@nuscenes-canbus-readme; @nuscenes-canbus-linear-accel-specific-force-local-2026-03-26; @nuscenes-canbus-local-2026-03-26]

## Pose samples after IMU end

### Observed pattern

In `616` of the `979` scenes, one final pose sample has a timestamp greater than the timestamp of the final IMU sample. There are never two or more pose samples after the final IMU sample.

The largest such pose-over-IMU gap is `34,654 us`.

### Runtime handling

`IMU-GPS-ESKF` treats these single trailing pose entries as extraneous and never uses them at runtime.

### Effect on synthetic GPS generation

This matters only during synthetic GPS generation. There are `110` scenes where the GPS generation algorithm would otherwise have used one of these `616` trailing pose samples.
